Section heading: Our use of cookies

**Session 2022-23**

chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by the Antisemitism Policy Trust (OSB23)**

This evidence is compiled and authored by the Antisemitism Policy Trust, a charitable organisation working to educate decision makers about antisemitism. The collaborative submission also comprises expert contributions provided by other bodies, either representative of the Jewish community, or working closely with it, and/or against antisemitism, specifically the Community Security Trust, Jewish Leadership Council and Holocaust Educational Trust.

**1\. Antisemitism Online:**

Antisemitism online is a serious and continual threat to Jewish people in the UK and across the world. This type of hate not only has a negative impact on its targets, but it toxifies civil discourse and can ultimately negatively impact democratic values and freedoms. Antisemitism online manifests in many ways, from dedicated websites housing and developing antisemitic conspiracy theories, to phenomenon centered on emergent technologies, for example so-called ‘Zoombombing’ whereby perpetrators of anti-Jewish hate join Jewish communal public meetings and target racist abuse at other attendees.
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**Written evidence submitted by the Antisemitism Policy Trust (OSB23)**

This evidence is compiled and authored by the Antisemitism Policy Trust, a charitable organisation working to educate decision makers about antisemitism. The collaborative submission also comprises expert contributions provided by other bodies, either representative of the Jewish community, or working closely with it, and/or against antisemitism, specifically the Community Security Trust, Jewish Leadership Council and Holocaust Educational Trust.

**1\. Antisemitism Online:**

Antisemitism online is a serious and continual threat to Jewish people in the UK and across the world. This type of hate not only has a negative impact on its targets, but it toxifies civil discourse and can ultimately negatively impact democratic values and freedoms. Antisemitism online manifests in many ways, from dedicated websites housing and developing antisemitic conspiracy theories, to phenomenon centered on emergent technologies, for example so-called ‘Zoombombing’ whereby perpetrators of anti-Jewish hate join Jewish communal public meetings and target racist abuse at other attendees.

chunks.text: can ultimately negatively impact democratic values and freedoms. Antisemitism online manifests in many ways, from dedicated websites housing and developing antisemitic conspiracy theories, to phenomenon centered on emergent technologies, for example so-called ‘Zoombombing’ whereby perpetrators of anti-Jewish hate join Jewish communal public meetings and target racist abuse at other attendees. The Community Security Trust (CST) which holds one of, if not the most accurate dataset on antisemitism in the world, reports that up to 40% of antisemitic incidents in the UK over the past several years have occurred online, although the CST only details incidents where the victim and/or perpetrator is based in the UK, where incidents have been proactively reported to them, and repeat attacks on an individual constitute one incident. If the CST were to proactively search for antisemitic incidents and discourse online, it would be a never-ending process.\[1\] 552 online antisemitic incidents were recorded by CST in 2021.\[2\] This is the third-highest annual total for online incidents ever reported. As the organisation rightly pointed out: "the online space provides antisemites with the opportunity to share political, ideological and extremist ideas, both simple and nuanced, with the security of anonymity and distance from their target. Abuse and threats can be preserved on these forums, spread across the world in an instant and reach their victim easily, often without meaningful ramifications for the perpetrator"
chunks.display_text: The Community Security Trust (CST) which holds one of, if not the most accurate dataset on antisemitism in the world, reports that up to 40% of antisemitic incidents in the UK over the past several years have occurred online, although the CST only details incidents where the victim and/or perpetrator is based in the UK, where incidents have been proactively reported to them, and repeat attacks on an individual constitute one incident. If the CST were to proactively search for antisemitic incidents and discourse online, it would be a never-ending process.\[1\] 552 online antisemitic incidents were recorded by CST in 2021.\[2\] This is the third-highest annual total for online incidents ever reported. As the organisation rightly pointed out: "the online space provides antisemites with the opportunity to share political, ideological and extremist ideas, both simple and nuanced, with the security of anonymity and distance from their target. Abuse and threats can be preserved on these forums, spread across the world in an instant and reach their victim easily, often without meaningful ramifications for the perpetrator"

chunks.text: opportunity to share political, ideological and extremist ideas, both simple and nuanced, with the security of anonymity and distance from their target. Abuse and threats can be preserved on these forums, spread across the world in an instant and reach their victim easily, often without meaningful ramifications for the perpetrator" Despite positive engagement with many of the world’s largest social media companies, we remain concerned that insufficient enforcement of their policies allows the spread of antisemitic hate on Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, YouTube, TikTok.\[3\] \[4\] In the case of small er or so-called ‘alternative’ platforms, including and specifically those that play host to individuals expressing extreme ideologies, for example on the far-right or in the so-called manosphere, few rules exist and antisemitism is widespread. In these spaces, antisemitic rhetoric is used to incite violent hatred o f, and radicalise people against Jews. Antisemitism on these platforms has inspired physical a ssault s and terrorism against Jews across the globe, and these platforms have been used to signal upcoming violent and deadly antisemitic attacks.\[5\] Online platforms have also been used for planning co \- ordinated online campaigns against Jewish people, including those in public life.\[6\] The available data underlines the severity of the situation. The Antisemitism Policy Trust and CST, working with the Woolf Institute, found that there are approximately two antisemitic tweets per year, for every Jewish person in the UK.\[7\] When the rapper Richard Kylea Cowie Jr (Wiley) engaged in an antisemitic rant on the platform, including calling Jews racists, making a comparison to the KKK, and alleging control of the police, the platform’s own "impressions" data showed his 599 tweets on 24th and 25th July received 306 million impressions. Even refined estimates, using more developed technologies, suggest the number of impressions is as great as 47 million.
chunks.display_text: Despite positive engagement with many of the world’s largest social media companies, we remain concerned that insufficient enforcement of their policies allows the spread of antisemitic hate on Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, YouTube, TikTok.\[3\] \[4\] In the case of small er or so-called ‘alternative’ platforms, including and specifically those that play host to individuals expressing extreme ideologies, for example on the far-right or in the so-called manosphere, few rules exist and antisemitism is widespread. In these spaces, antisemitic rhetoric is used to incite violent hatred o f, and radicalise people against Jews. Antisemitism on these platforms has inspired physical a ssault s and terrorism against Jews across the globe, and these platforms have been used to signal upcoming violent and deadly antisemitic attacks.\[5\] Online platforms have also been used for planning co \- ordinated online campaigns against Jewish people, including those in public life.\[6\] The available data underlines the severity of the situation. The Antisemitism Policy Trust and CST, working with the Woolf Institute, found that there are approximately two antisemitic tweets per year, for every Jewish person in the UK.\[7\] When the rapper Richard Kylea Cowie Jr (Wiley) engaged in an antisemitic rant on the platform, including calling Jews racists, making a comparison to the KKK, and alleging control of the police, the platform’s own "impressions" data showed his 599 tweets on 24th and 25th July received 306 million impressions. Even refined estimates, using more developed technologies, suggest the number of impressions is as great as 47 million.

chunks.text: Jews racists, making a comparison to the KKK, and alleging control of the police, the platform’s own "impressions" data showed his 599 tweets on 24th and 25th July received 306 million impressions. Even refined estimates, using more developed technologies, suggest the number of impressions is as great as 47 million. We support the general aims of the Online Safety Bill and welcome Government’s (and indeed opposition parties) intention to combat antisemitism in the digital sphere \[8\], we do however have a number of proposals for strengthening the Bill which we would like the Committee to consider. **2\. Small, High Harm Platforms** As currently drafted, the Bill will enable Ofcom to place companies into categories, with different levels of regulatory burden. The determination for categorisation, contained in Schedule 10 of the Bill, is size and ‘functionality’ (relating to the mechanisms of a platform or service, in Clause 186). As numerous parliamentarians made clear during the Second Reading of the Bill, many platforms that host large volumes of extremely harmful material might be exempt from appropriate regulatory capture.
chunks.display_text: We support the general aims of the Online Safety Bill and welcome Government’s (and indeed opposition parties) intention to combat antisemitism in the digital sphere \[8\], we do however have a number of proposals for strengthening the Bill which we would like the Committee to consider. **2\. Small, High Harm Platforms** As currently drafted, the Bill will enable Ofcom to place companies into categories, with different levels of regulatory burden. The determination for categorisation, contained in Schedule 10 of the Bill, is size and ‘functionality’ (relating to the mechanisms of a platform or service, in Clause 186). As numerous parliamentarians made clear during the Second Reading of the Bill, many platforms that host large volumes of extremely harmful material might be exempt from appropriate regulatory capture.

chunks.text: Schedule 10 of the Bill, is size and ‘functionality’ (relating to the mechanisms of a platform or service, in Clause 186). As numerous parliamentarians made clear during the Second Reading of the Bill, many platforms that host large volumes of extremely harmful material might be exempt from appropriate regulatory capture. Andrew Percy MP gave a specific example: "these small platforms are a haven for white supremacists, incels, conspiracy theorists and antisemites; it is where they gather, converse and share and spew their hate. An example of that is a post from the so-called anti-Jewish meme repository on the platform Gab which showed a picture of goblins, in this instance the usual grotesque representation of those age-old Jewish physical stereotypes, alongside the phrase, "Are you ready to die in another Jewish war, Goyim \[9\]?" That is the sort of stuff that is on these small platforms, and it is not rare; we see it all over. Indeed, many of these small platforms exist purely to spew such hate, but at present, despite the many measures in the Bill that I support, these sites will be sifted by Ofcom into two major categories based on their size and functionality."
chunks.display_text: Andrew Percy MP gave a specific example: "these small platforms are a haven for white supremacists, incels, conspiracy theorists and antisemites; it is where they gather, converse and share and spew their hate. An example of that is a post from the so-called anti-Jewish meme repository on the platform Gab which showed a picture of goblins, in this instance the usual grotesque representation of those age-old Jewish physical stereotypes, alongside the phrase, "Are you ready to die in another Jewish war, Goyim \[9\]?" That is the sort of stuff that is on these small platforms, and it is not rare; we see it all over. Indeed, many of these small platforms exist purely to spew such hate, but at present, despite the many measures in the Bill that I support, these sites will be sifted by Ofcom into two major categories based on their size and functionality."

chunks.text: is not rare; we see it all over. Indeed, many of these small platforms exist purely to spew such hate, but at present, despite the many measures in the Bill that I support, these sites will be sifted by Ofcom into two major categories based on their size and functionality." The Community Security Trust has outlined in detail \[10\] some of the most shocking and violent materials on these small, high harm sites, and whilst illegal material has been present, much of that content is legal but harmful (and would be addressed in other environments, such as a football ground, cinema or on TV/radio). That lawful material can and has transferred to more mainstream platforms and has influenced real world events. The Antisemitism Policy Trust briefing on the connection between online and offline harms details how antisemitic terrorism, like the deadly attack in a synagogue in Pittsburgh, and deadly Islamophobic attacks, like the Christchurch Mosque shootings, were carried out by men who were, at least in part, radicalised online and whom signaled their intent to attack online, and in some cases sought to livestream their attacks online.\[11\] The recent tragic murders in Buffalo follow a similar pattern.\[12\] The duty of care approach established in the Bill is predicated on risk assessment. If size allows platforms to dodge the entry criteria for managing high risk, there is a hole in the regime.
chunks.display_text: The Community Security Trust has outlined in detail \[10\] some of the most shocking and violent materials on these small, high harm sites, and whilst illegal material has been present, much of that content is legal but harmful (and would be addressed in other environments, such as a football ground, cinema or on TV/radio). That lawful material can and has transferred to more mainstream platforms and has influenced real world events. The Antisemitism Policy Trust briefing on the connection between online and offline harms details how antisemitic terrorism, like the deadly attack in a synagogue in Pittsburgh, and deadly Islamophobic attacks, like the Christchurch Mosque shootings, were carried out by men who were, at least in part, radicalised online and whom signaled their intent to attack online, and in some cases sought to livestream their attacks online.\[11\] The recent tragic murders in Buffalo follow a similar pattern.\[12\] The duty of care approach established in the Bill is predicated on risk assessment. If size allows platforms to dodge the entry criteria for managing high risk, there is a hole in the regime.

chunks.text: sought to livestream their attacks online.\[11\] The recent tragic murders in Buffalo follow a similar pattern.\[12\] The duty of care approach established in the Bill is predicated on risk assessment. If size allows platforms to dodge the entry criteria for managing high risk, there is a hole in the regime. There may be different ways to address this policy lacuna. For example, the Bill already includes the term ‘characteristics’ (in clause 83 (12)). This broader term should allow Ofcom the breadth to capture small, high harm platforms in its category determination. An alternative might be a new Section 10 1(1) (c) with a fallback option for Ofcom to add platforms presenting a significant risk of harm (irrespective of size) into the first Category. This would ensure that not all companies are automatically dragged into the arrangement, thus protecting numerous non-harmful small platforms and start-ups. The Trust has additional concerns about the ability of Ofcom to swiftly re- categorise platforms, and in relation to the Secretary of State’s powers to over-ride risk-based assessments provided by Of com. In general, we believe these powers undermine the independent nature of the regulatory regime, and risk future misuse. **3** **Search (Address for Legal Harms)** User-to-user and Search Services are treated differently in the Online Safety Bill. As it stands, Google and Microsoft Bing, and voice search assistants including Amazon’s Alexa and Apple’s Siri, will be exempted from having to risk assess their systems in relation to addressing legal harms to adults arising through systems design or operation, for example through their algorithmic indexing, promotion, and autocomplete features in search bars.
chunks.display_text: There may be different ways to address this policy lacuna. For example, the Bill already includes the term ‘characteristics’ (in clause 83 (12)). This broader term should allow Ofcom the breadth to capture small, high harm platforms in its category determination. An alternative might be a new Section 10 1(1) (c) with a fallback option for Ofcom to add platforms presenting a significant risk of harm (irrespective of size) into the first Category. This would ensure that not all companies are automatically dragged into the arrangement, thus protecting numerous non-harmful small platforms and start-ups. The Trust has additional concerns about the ability of Ofcom to swiftly re- categorise platforms, and in relation to the Secretary of State’s powers to over-ride risk-based assessments provided by Of com. In general, we believe these powers undermine the independent nature of the regulatory regime, and risk future misuse. **3** **Search (Address for Legal Harms)** User-to-user and Search Services are treated differently in the Online Safety Bill. As it stands, Google and Microsoft Bing, and voice search assistants including Amazon’s Alexa and Apple’s Siri, will be exempted from having to risk assess their systems in relation to addressing legal harms to adults arising through systems design or operation, for example through their algorithmic indexing, promotion, and autocomplete features in search bars.

chunks.text: and Microsoft Bing, and voice search assistants including Amazon’s Alexa and Apple’s Siri, will be exempted from having to risk assess their systems in relation to addressing legal harms to adults arising through systems design or operation, for example through their algorithmic indexing, promotion, and autocomplete features in search bars. The Antisemitism Policy Trust and CST have demonstrated how Google’s services have led to harm.\[13\] \[14\] Google ’s autocomplete feature previously prompted users towards the search ‘Are Jews…. evil?’, and its ‘ SafeSearch ’ does not work in relation to filtering antisemitic image content. Microsoft Bing ’s autocomplete feature directed users to the search term ‘Jews are b\*\*\*\*\*\*s’. Amazon’s Alexa service directed people to both antisemitic and anti-Muslim results,for example in response to questions about whether the Jews run the world,\[15\] before it was embarrassed into changing its systems, In its report on the draft Online Safety Bill, the parliamentary joint committee recognised that search engines were not solely passive entities: "They rely on algorithmic ranking and often include automatic design features like autocomplete and voice activated searches that can steer people in the direction of content that puts them or others at risk of harm" …. "It is reasonable to expect them to come under the Bill’s requirements and, in particular, for them to conduct risk assessments of their system design to ensure it mitigates rather exacerbates risks of harm".
chunks.display_text: The Antisemitism Policy Trust and CST have demonstrated how Google’s services have led to harm.\[13\] \[14\] Google ’s autocomplete feature previously prompted users towards the search ‘Are Jews…. evil?’, and its ‘ SafeSearch ’ does not work in relation to filtering antisemitic image content. Microsoft Bing ’s autocomplete feature directed users to the search term ‘Jews are b\*\*\*\*\*\*s’. Amazon’s Alexa service directed people to both antisemitic and anti-Muslim results,for example in response to questions about whether the Jews run the world,\[15\] before it was embarrassed into changing its systems, In its report on the draft Online Safety Bill, the parliamentary joint committee recognised that search engines were not solely passive entities: "They rely on algorithmic ranking and often include automatic design features like autocomplete and voice activated searches that can steer people in the direction of content that puts them or others at risk of harm" …. "It is reasonable to expect them to come under the Bill’s requirements and, in particular, for them to conduct risk assessments of their system design to ensure it mitigates rather exacerbates risks of harm".

chunks.text: people in the direction of content that puts them or others at risk of harm" …. "It is reasonable to expect them to come under the Bill’s requirements and, in particular, for them to conduct risk assessments of their system design to ensure it mitigates rather exacerbates risks of harm". Search services are private, ubiquitous, can and do direct people to harm. Failure to require proper risk assessment is a mistake. There are several associated gaps in thinking, as Professor Lorna Woods of Essex University has pointed out to the Trust, including a lack of consideration of how adverts might be used via search services to cause harm, and in relation to the workability of the terms ‘encountering’ and ‘user content’ when considering harm in the context of Search service systems. We recommend amending the Bill to add a risk assessment, and policy response with accompanying oversight. We recommend that an additional section on risk assessments protecting adults be added to the requirements for regulated search services, similar to S ection 23 (5) in relation to illegal content. **4 Duties of Care: Countervailing Duties**

There are several associated gaps in thinking, as Professor Lorna Woods of Essex University has pointed out to the Trust, including a lack of consideration of how adverts might be used via search services to cause harm, and in relation to the workability of the terms ‘encountering’ and ‘user content’ when considering harm in the context of Search service systems. We recommend amending the Bill to add a risk assessment, and policy response with accompanying oversight. We recommend that an additional section on risk assessments protecting adults be added to the requirements for regulated search services, similar to S ection 23 (5) in relation to illegal content. **4 Duties of Care: Countervailing Duties**

chunks.text: the Bill to add a risk assessment, and policy response with accompanying oversight. We recommend that an additional section on risk assessments protecting adults be added to the requirements for regulated search services, similar to S ection 23 (5) in relation to illegal content. **4 Duties of Care: Countervailing Duties** There are two duties applicable to platforms in Category 1 about which we have concerns. According to Section 15 of Chapter 2, relevant platforms must protect content of democratic importance which is defined in (6b) (b) as "content that is or appears to be specifically intended to contribute to democratic political debate in the United Kingdom or a part or area of the United Kingdom", and journalistic content (Section 16). Whilst we support the underlying principles behind these duties, and understand the concerns which gave rise to them, the lack of definitional text lends the Bill to abuse by extremists seeking to actively undermine the democratic process by disseminating hateful and racist material, disinformation and other harmful content. The UN’s four Special Rapporteurs on freedom of expression have made clear that political speech can and does cause harm.\[16\] We also know, including from work carried out by British parliamentarians, that certain groups can feel marginalised, ostracized or otherwise left of our democratic discourse \[17\]. Though framed as seeking to protect democratically important content, rather than protecting democratic spaces, the Bill appears to be avoiding censorship. This is a laudable effort but further definition, detail, or at a minimum, a review mechanism in or accompanying the Bill is required.
chunks.display_text: There are two duties applicable to platforms in Category 1 about which we have concerns. According to Section 15 of Chapter 2, relevant platforms must protect content of democratic importance which is defined in (6b) (b) as "content that is or appears to be specifically intended to contribute to democratic political debate in the United Kingdom or a part or area of the United Kingdom", and journalistic content (Section 16). Whilst we support the underlying principles behind these duties, and understand the concerns which gave rise to them, the lack of definitional text lends the Bill to abuse by extremists seeking to actively undermine the democratic process by disseminating hateful and racist material, disinformation and other harmful content. The UN’s four Special Rapporteurs on freedom of expression have made clear that political speech can and does cause harm.\[16\] We also know, including from work carried out by British parliamentarians, that certain groups can feel marginalised, ostracized or otherwise left of our democratic discourse \[17\]. Though framed as seeking to protect democratically important content, rather than protecting democratic spaces, the Bill appears to be avoiding censorship. This is a laudable effort but further definition, detail, or at a minimum, a review mechanism in or accompanying the Bill is required.

chunks.text: left of our democratic discourse \[17\]. Though framed as seeking to protect democratically important content, rather than protecting democratic spaces, the Bill appears to be avoiding censorship. This is a laudable effort but further definition, detail, or at a minimum, a review mechanism in or accompanying the Bill is required. The protective duty in relation to journalistic content presents additional concerns. The exemption for " recogni s ed news publishers" in 49 (2) and definition of such publishers in 50 (2) should include more explicit protections. We share concerns raised by others that there is no minimum standard or quality assurance for the "standards code" such publications are required to abide by in the Bill, nor is there any quality assurance for complaints handling processes that such bodies must have in place. The result is that s ites seeking to intentionally spread harmful misinformation or anti-Jewish r acis m (and indeed individuals republishing it) would benefit from the proposed exemption. There are examples of far-right activists self-identifying as journalists, and news companies like InfoWars, Rebel Media or Urban Sccop which spread hateful and dangerous conspiracy theories that could easily benefit from the exemptions as drafted. It is possible to envisage a scenario in which far-right citizen journalists are legitimised by winning a complaint and thereby ‘proving’ they are indeed journalists. We are also not convinced by the explanations offered to the Trust and others by officials, that platforms will have to perform a balancing act in respect of harms (especially given some platforms are designed for harm). We believe the opportunity for bad actors to take advantage of the Bill, as drafted, is too great and that further definition and specificity is required.

We share concerns raised by others that there is no minimum standard or quality assurance for the "standards code" such publications are required to abide by in the Bill, nor is there any quality assurance for complaints handling processes that such bodies must have in place. It is possible to envisage a scenario in which far-right citizen journalists are legitimised by winning a complaint and thereby ‘proving’ they are indeed journalists. We are also not convinced by the explanations offered to the Trust and others by officials, that platforms will have to perform a balancing act in respect of harms (especially given some platforms are designed for harm). We believe the opportunity for bad actors to take advantage of the Bill, as drafted, is too great and that further definition and specificity is required.

We believe the opportunity for bad actors to take advantage of the Bill, as drafted, is too great and that further definition and specificity is required. Clause 39(2)(d) exempts "comments and reviews on provider content" (later defined in Clause 39(5)) from the requirements set out for user-to-user services in Clause 2 (1). For over a decade, The Trust has worked with publishers, Governments, IPSO and others to address concerns about racism on major newspaper website comments boards. For example, as secretariat to the All-Party Parliamentary Group Against Antisemitism, the Trust worked with the Department of Communities and Local Government (now DLUHC) towards a guide delivered by the Society of Editors in 2014, which was inspired by discussion of this form of harm \[18\], and together with members of the All-Party Parliamentary Group Against Antisemitism, have repeatedly pressed IPSO to revise its Editors’ Code to include redress for group discrimination.\[19\] The comments on such sites, which continue to proliferate and be inconsistently addressed, are not effectively handled by press regulators, or indeed many publications despite many millions of impressions they attract.\[20\] **5****. Anonymity** Part 4 (Section 57) of the Bill introduces a new duty on Category 1 entities, in Chapter 1, to include a verification option for users of relevant services. In an earlier section of the Bill (Part 3, Section 14) Category 1 companies are required to give users the ability to "filter out non-verified users", as part of the "user empowerment duties".
chunks.display_text: Clause 39(2)(d) exempts "comments and reviews on provider content" (later defined in Clause 39(5)) from the requirements set out for user-to-user services in Clause 2 (1). For over a decade, The Trust has worked with publishers, Governments, IPSO and others to address concerns about racism on major newspaper website comments boards. For example, as secretariat to the All-Party Parliamentary Group Against Antisemitism, the Trust worked with the Department of Communities and Local Government (now DLUHC) towards a guide delivered by the Society of Editors in 2014, which was inspired by discussion of this form of harm \[18\], and together with members of the All-Party Parliamentary Group Against Antisemitism, have repeatedly pressed IPSO to revise its Editors’ Code to include redress for group discrimination.\[19\] The comments on such sites, which continue to proliferate and be inconsistently addressed, are not effectively handled by press regulators, or indeed many publications despite many millions of impressions they attract.\[20\] **5****. Anonymity** Part 4 (Section 57) of the Bill introduces a new duty on Category 1 entities, in Chapter 1, to include a verification option for users of relevant services. In an earlier section of the Bill (Part 3, Section 14) Category 1 companies are required to give users the ability to "filter out non-verified users", as part of the "user empowerment duties".

chunks.text: on Category 1 entities, in Chapter 1, to include a verification option for users of relevant services. In an earlier section of the Bill (Part 3, Section 14) Category 1 companies are required to give users the ability to "filter out non-verified users", as part of the "user empowerment duties". The Trust has set out our position on this issue previously, detailing the impact of anonymity on Jewish communities online.\[21\] We also recognise the importance of anonymity to individuals in different contexts. It is the Trust’s position that it should be the responsibility of a given platform to determine the degree of anonymity it wishes to offer users, though there should be a risk-assessed approach, with incentives against hateful content, and severe penalties for anonymous abuse. In our view, if a crime or a libel has been committed in the UK on regulated technologies and companies in scope cannot or will not provide proof of identity, where a magistrate’s court order demands it (subject to an appropriate burden of proof), then a range of options should be considered. The Trust believes that the civil or criminal liability should pass to the platform itself, and fines or other corrective measures could be put in place. We were very pleased to see the recommendations of the joint committee reflect some of our proposals, including concerns about a lack of traceability by law enforcement. We supported the call for a specific risk assessment on safety by design, in relation to anonymity and law enforcement, and further details to be required within a statutory Code of Practice issued by Ofcom.
chunks.display_text: The Trust has set out our position on this issue previously, detailing the impact of anonymity on Jewish communities online.\[21\] We also recognise the importance of anonymity to individuals in different contexts. It is the Trust’s position that it should be the responsibility of a given platform to determine the degree of anonymity it wishes to offer users, though there should be a risk-assessed approach, with incentives against hateful content, and severe penalties for anonymous abuse. In our view, if a crime or a libel has been committed in the UK on regulated technologies and companies in scope cannot or will not provide proof of identity, where a magistrate’s court order demands it (subject to an appropriate burden of proof), then a range of options should be considered. The Trust believes that the civil or criminal liability should pass to the platform itself, and fines or other corrective measures could be put in place. We were very pleased to see the recommendations of the joint committee reflect some of our proposals, including concerns about a lack of traceability by law enforcement. We supported the call for a specific risk assessment on safety by design, in relation to anonymity and law enforcement, and further details to be required within a statutory Code of Practice issued by Ofcom.

chunks.text: reflect some of our proposals, including concerns about a lack of traceability by law enforcement. We supported the call for a specific risk assessment on safety by design, in relation to anonymity and law enforcement, and further details to be required within a statutory Code of Practice issued by Ofcom. In the current version of the Bill, Ofcom is required to produce guidance on compliance with the User Verification Duty, and we believe that, at a minimum, this requirement should include reference to users with protected Characteristics under the Equality Act 2010, and reference to disclosure to police in respect of criminal investigations. We would likely support amendment s seeking to strengthen the Bill in this regard. **6****. Penalties** In its report on the Draft Online Safety Bill, the House of Commons Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee made the following finding and recommendation: "The Antisemitism Policy Trust has noted that while the Bill details how Ofcom should publish decisions for failures against the duty of care, it provides no such provisions for mandatory breach notices for service providers. Such transparency would theoretically incentivise compliance while providing greater transparency for users of a service when a decision is made by Ofcom. We recommend that the Government include a provision in the Bill to mandate publication of a breach notice by a service. This should include details of their breaches against the duty of care and be available to view on the platform." The Government’s response to the committee explained that it considered the requirement on Ofcom to publish details of its enforcement activity sufficient transparency. This is potentially a get out of jail free card for platforms.
chunks.display_text: In the current version of the Bill, Ofcom is required to produce guidance on compliance with the User Verification Duty, and we believe that, at a minimum, this requirement should include reference to users with protected Characteristics under the Equality Act 2010, and reference to disclosure to police in respect of criminal investigations. We would likely support amendment s seeking to strengthen the Bill in this regard. **6****. Penalties** In its report on the Draft Online Safety Bill, the House of Commons Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee made the following finding and recommendation: "The Antisemitism Policy Trust has noted that while the Bill details how Ofcom should publish decisions for failures against the duty of care, it provides no such provisions for mandatory breach notices for service providers. Such transparency would theoretically incentivise compliance while providing greater transparency for users of a service when a decision is made by Ofcom. We recommend that the Government include a provision in the Bill to mandate publication of a breach notice by a service. This should include details of their breaches against the duty of care and be available to view on the platform." The Government’s response to the committee explained that it considered the requirement on Ofcom to publish details of its enforcement activity sufficient transparency. This is potentially a get out of jail free card for platforms.

chunks.text: their breaches against the duty of care and be available to view on the platform." The Government’s response to the committee explained that it considered the requirement on Ofcom to publish details of its enforcement activity sufficient transparency. This is potentially a get out of jail free card for platforms. A simple amendment to Chapter 6 of this part of the Bill, possibly in Section 128 or a new Section 129 could ensure p roviders of regulated services must, in consultation with Ofcom, publish details of a penalty notice. **7\. Risk Assessments and Priority Content Harmful to Adults** Ofcom has some sensible requirements placed upon it in Chapter 3, including in relation to undertaking risk assessments and compiling a risk register, lists of risks grouped into profiles according to service type. However, in clause 83 (6) it states in preparing the risk profiles which relate to the risk of harm mentioned in subsection (1) (c) \[risk of harm to adults\], "OFCOM must not take into account anything relating to non-designated content that is harmful to adults.". This leaves a lot to chance, given designated content is not due to be specified until secondary legislation is introduction, it also enables Ofcom to identify a harm but then disregard it in relation to the risks presented by the services it is regulating. There are various ways in which this might be addressed. At the very least, there should be an exemption built in so that non-designated harms, where there is demonstrable impact, the threshold for which should be defined in the Bill, can be taken into account. Better still, this constraining text could simply be removed.

Risk Assessments and Priority Content Harmful to Adults** Ofcom has some sensible requirements placed upon it in Chapter 3, including in relation to undertaking risk assessments and compiling a risk register, lists of risks grouped into profiles according to service type. However, in clause 83 (6) it states in preparing the risk profiles which relate to the risk of harm mentioned in subsection (1) (c) \[risk of harm to adults\], "OFCOM must not take into account anything relating to non-designated content that is harmful to adults.". This leaves a lot to chance, given designated content is not due to be specified until secondary legislation is introduction, it also enables Ofcom to identify a harm but then disregard it in relation to the risks presented by the services it is regulating. There are various ways in which this might be addressed. At the very least, there should be an exemption built in so that non-designated harms, where there is demonstrable impact, the threshold for which should be defined in the Bill, can be taken into account. Better still, this constraining text could simply be removed.

chunks.text: in which this might be addressed. At the very least, there should be an exemption built in so that non-designated harms, where there is demonstrable impact, the threshold for which should be defined in the Bill, can be taken into account. Better still, this constraining text could simply be removed. An amendment to ensure priority legal harms are listed in the Bill would however be very welcome. The Government has already established a principle for listing defined harms, and there is no reason therefore to treat legal harms differently for the purposes of the Bill. This would reduce uncertainty for victims’ groups, for the regulator and others. The list might have reference to existing legal protections, as encouraged by the joint committee on the draft Bill, for example those set out in the Equality Act. **8\. Super Complaints** We are pleased to see confirmation of the Super Complaint function in Part 8, Chapter 2 of the Bill, through which substantial evidence of systematic issues affecting large numbers or specific groups of people can be heard by Ofcom. We are however significantly concerned that Section 140 (3) specifies ‘eligible entities’ must meet criteria specified in regulations made by the Secretary of State. This leaves another important area of specificity to secondary legislation. We would at the very least expect Secretary of State to establish or indicate some basic principles for eligibility during the passage of the Bill so that organisations like the Antisemitism Policy Trust and Community Security Trust can be reassured we will be heard on matters pertaining to antisemitism, on which we are expert. **9\. Supply Chains**

This would reduce uncertainty for victims’ groups, for the regulator and others. The list might have reference to existing legal protections, as encouraged by the joint committee on the draft Bill, for example those set out in the Equality Act. **8\. Super Complaints** We are pleased to see confirmation of the Super Complaint function in Part 8, Chapter 2 of the Bill, through which substantial evidence of systematic issues affecting large numbers or specific groups of people can be heard by Ofcom. We are however significantly concerned that Section 140 (3) specifies ‘eligible entities’ must meet criteria specified in regulations made by the Secretary of State. This leaves another important area of specificity to secondary legislation. We would at the very least expect Secretary of State to establish or indicate some basic principles for eligibility during the passage of the Bill so that organisations like the Antisemitism Policy Trust and Community Security Trust can be reassured we will be heard on matters pertaining to antisemitism, on which we are expert. **9\. Supply Chains**

chunks.text: Secretary of State to establish or indicate some basic principles for eligibility during the passage of the Bill so that organisations like the Antisemitism Policy Trust and Community Security Trust can be reassured we will be heard on matters pertaining to antisemitism, on which we are expert. **9\. Supply Chains** Platforms, particularly those supporting user-to-user generated content, employ services from third parties. In the past, this has included Twitter explaining that racist Gifs were not its own but provided by another service. YouTube found it difficult to give precise figures for its moderator team given several moderators were employed or operated under third parties. There are examples in UK legislation, for example the Bribery Act, in which a company is liable if anyone performing services for or on the company’s behalf, is found culpable of specific actions. We recommend liability be introduced for platforms failing to ensure associated parties, considered to be part of a regulated service, perhaps subject to guidance from Ofcom, help to fulfil and abide by relevant duties. Whilst we acknowledge the definition of user-to-user service in the Bill, we remain concerned that there might be wilful or other misinterpretations of this. **10\. Restrictive Covenant** The Trust believes that the Bill should include a restrictive covenant on senior government and Ofcom officials with direct responsibility for any platform within the regulatory ambit of the Bill. The revolving door between Government and social media platforms can be sinister and certainly undermines public trust.
